comments.content:
[IMAGE:0]Midnight is just past, and so it's time to launch a new Magic Monday. Ask me anything about occultism, and with certain exceptions noted below, any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. Please note:  Any question or comment received after that point will not get an answer, and in fact will just be deleted.  If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 341,928th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.2 of The Magic Monday FAQ here. 

Also: I will not be putting through or answering any more questions about practicing magic around children. I've answered those in simple declarative sentences in the FAQ. If you read the FAQ and don't think your question has been answered, read it again. If that doesn't help, consider remedial reading classes; yes, it really is as simple and straightforward as the FAQ says.  And further:  I've decided that questions about getting goodies from spirits are also permanently off topic here. The point of occultism is to develop your own capacities, not to try to bully or wheedle other beings into doing things for you. I've discussed this in a post on my blog.

The image? I field a lot of questions about my books these days, so I've decided to do little capsule summaries of them here, one per week.  This was my fifty-ninth published book, and those of you who aren't Lovecraft fans can breathe a sigh of relief now, as we're out of tentacle territory at long last. The Dolmen Arch is a study course in Druidical occultism, and it came about in a very odd way. The Ancient Order of Druids in America (AODA), the order I more or less rescued from the dumpster in 2003, had been through political struggles a decade further back and a lot of people had bailed out. After I became Grand Archdruid, since I hadn't been involved in any of the fussing, some of these people contacted me or simply mailed me packages with notes amounting to, "Now that you're Grand Archdruid you ought to have this." What "this" amounted to was a very mixed bag, but in one package, tucked into a bunch of unrelated papers on French occult orders, were parts of a couple of lessons of an old American Druid correspondence course from between the two world wars.

I spent a while being annoyed that the rest of the course was nowhere to be found. Then it occurred to me that I could reverse engineer it without too much difficulty, since I'd been able to figure out some of the important influences on the course and I also had a wealth of other twentieth century Druid material. The result was a hefty seven-unit correspondence course. I taught it privately to students for a while, then arranged to publish it with Azoth Press in a very nice fine edition. When that sold out, Azoth decided to bring out a trade paper edition, which is still available and selling steadily. Apparently there's a demand for fussy, richly philosophical, old-fashioned Druid training. This is the first volume of two. If you're in the market for that, you can get copies here in the US and from your favorite online retailer elsewhere.

Buy Me A Coffee

Ko-Fi

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use either of the links above to access my online tip jar; Buymeacoffee is good for small tips, Ko-Fi is better for larger ones. (I used to use PayPal but they developed an allergy to free speech, so I've developed an allergy to them.) If you're interested in political and economic astrology, or simply prefer to use a subscription service to support your favorite authors, you can find my Patreon page here and my SubscribeStar page here. 
 
[IMAGE:1]I've also had quite a few people over the years ask me where they should buy my books, and here's the answer. Bookshop.org is an alternative online bookstore that supports local bookstores and authors, which a certain gargantuan corporation doesn't, and I have a shop there, which you can check out here. Please consider patronizing it if you'd like to purchase any of my books online.

And don't forget to look up your Pangalactic New Age Soul Signature at CosmicOom.com.

With that said, have at it!

***This Magic Monday is now closed, and no further comments will be put through. See you next week!***

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hello and thank you for hosting.

In Circles of Power you go over the Greater Pentagram ritual and how it can be used to invoke or banish specific elemental forces.

What is the purpose of doing this, and what can be accomplished with it? For example, if I performed a greater invoking pentagram ritual of Fire on a regular basis, what kinds of effects would be likely? I’m intrigued by elemental work but I don’t have a good sense of what it is good for accomplishing, especially relative to things like astrological and sephiroth rituals. For whatever it’s worth I already do the lesser invoking and banishing rituals daily.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: You don't do these rituals independently. You do them as part of a specific magical working for a chosen intention. For example, you would perform the greater invoking pentagram ritual of Fire as part of a ritual to consecrate a talisman of the Fire element for some purpose, such as strengthening your will. You might also use it as part of a working in which you traveled to the elemental plane of Fire for the purpose of communing with the spirits of Fire and learning from them.

comments.title: Divination Offering Still Open and Yearly "Boast"
comments.content:
Good Evening,

A happy early New Years to all here! (And may some of your bleakness lift, JMG).

On the book, I'll use this opportunity to once again thank you, JMG, for the Dolmen Arch course, and to encourage anyone interested to give it a look. It's a lot of work, but I have found it of great worth over the last two years or so and am happily plugging away at the fifth grade of seven.

To Share:

1) For those of you who missed it last week, I'm offering divination (Runes or Ogham) through Tuesday (the end of the year). For anyone who already posted a question, I've done most of y'all's readings, but am delayed in getting the interpretations written up due to family holiday stuff. I'll have them up in the next few days. If you'd like to have me give a shot on giving some insight on a question, head over to my dreamwidth here and post your question: https://jprussell.dreamwidth.org/25825.html

2) As some of y'all may recall, for the past few years, I have followed a modified version of the Heathen custom of symbel. Typically, this involves getting together with fellow believers and passing around a sacred drink. Before taking a drink, each participant raises the horn and makes a toast. Typically, at least one round will be to the Gods, one to the ancestors, and any subsequent rounds will include "boasts" - not empty bragging, but rather making an oath to accomplish something worthwhile, or sharing success in something done. The goal is to entwine these deeds, done or to-be-done, with the luck and Wyrd (very roughly "fate") of the community represented by those present, and so it is a holy and weighty thing not to be taken lightly. Lacking a local Heathen community, I've made toasts here and invited any who wish to join me to do so.

Last year, I made an oath to write a book this year, and I've been working on writing up a book on the "Tally of the Bedes" (the "Heathen Rosary" I've posted about here before). While I wish I could say "it is done!" instead all I can say is "it will be done in the next two days." This first draft is shorter than it likely needs to be if I hope to get it published, but it is a whole book with everything needed to get started saying the "Tally of the Bedes" and to change it to better suit your own practice. I will wrap up this first draft before the end of the day Tuesday, and then likely edit heavily and expand in the first part of the year. So I'm calling this a success unless something major unforeseen comes up (in which case, I will be here next week to eat crow). I'll be putting up a more in-depth write-up on this and how my year went otherwise later this week.

To Ask: For anyone willing, kindly lend your luck and good wishes to my keeping my oath for 2025, and if you feel so moved, to add in an oath or boast of your own, with a toast:


Before the close || of the coming year,
A fee for my writing || will I fairly take,
Sharing my words, || sharply crafted,
And worthwhile to read, || that readily given
Are pennies hard-won, || Woden's gifts to gain.


(That is to say, by December 31st, 2025, I will have received payment for at least one piece of writing in some shape).

As always, thanks very much to JMG and those who read and comment.

My blessings to all who welcome them,
Jeff

To share: On Christmas morning I was doing my daily divination with the SGO, one card each to represent me, a situation I'd face, and the outcome. The reading:

The Pentagram - Power
Unity of the Primary Roots (Reversed) - Paradox
The Vesica Piscis - Union

The more I reflect on this unexpected gift, the more astounding it seems to me. It fills me with gratitude and amazement.

comments.title: R. Swinburne Clymer bio details
comments.content:
I understand that you are involved with the Fraternitas Rosae Crucis. I am trying to work out some basic biographical details of R. Swinburne Clymer--specifically, the dates of his two (I think) marriages, and which children he had during each. Can you help? I realize that Rosicrucians probably don't have such things memorized, but perhaps you could point me to sources.

In 1898, Clymer married Laura Edith Bowers (1881-1954), whom he divorced at some point to marry Gertrude Lucy Cosgrove (1892-1951). Genealogical websites credit him with as many as nine children, but I am unable to reconcile the proposed dates and mothers.

Laura Bowers’ tombstone (photo at https://www.findagrave.com/memorial/79691532/laura_edith_clymer ) gives the names of five children born between 1898 and 1914, none of whom survived to adulthood. Their fathers are not indicated. FamilySearch ( https://ancestors.familysearch.org/en/G4RG-YK5/delila-alverta-clymer-1898-1908 and https://ancestors.familysearch.org/en/G6DN-Z51/edith-arlene-clymer-1913-1913 ) credits her and Clymer with four more children, whose dates overlap with those of the other five, but Ancestry.com names Gertrude Cosgrove as the mother of Beverly P. Clymer (1905-1959) and Emerson Myron Clymer (1909-1983), Clymer’s successor as leader of the Fraternitas Rosae Crucis. See https://www.ancestry.com/genealogy/records/beverly-p-clymer-24-6pp7pw, and https://www.ancestry.com/genealogy/records/gertrude-lucy-cosgrove-24-10js11 (The former gives her Laura Bowers’ dates, hinting at wider problems of data-blending.)

Thank you for whatever clarity you can bring!

Have you contacted the other FRC at Soul.org? They ought to be able to help.

comments.title: My Birth Number
comments.content:
Last week, I read some of your posts on numerology, and I calculated that my birth number is 5 and my name number is 11.

The description of my birth number fits me pretty well, but my name number doesn't for the most part.

How would my birth number influence my later life? Your description in the post mentioned "restless seeking," so does that mean I'll be unable to achieve lasting fulfillment in this life?

And am I right in suspecting that the reason my name number description doesn't seem to describe me is that it conflicts with my destiny, which cannot be altered?

replies.title: Re: My Birth Number
replies.content:
One of the classic ways to misunderstand any divination is to pull one detail out of an interpretation, take it out of context, and fixate on that. Here's the whole passage:

"If your birth number is 5, the challenge you face in this incarnation is learning the lessons of freedom, for you will spend this life in restless seeking and you will always choose your own path. You are equipped for this challenge with abundant energy and overflowing interest in the unfamiliar; you are impatient with routine and prefer change and excitement. You tend toward the eccentric and the extreme, and you tend to have sudden intense enthusiasms which do not always last long. You are quick to accept new ideas and are genuinely interested in people, you do unexpected things and are constantly heading for new places, and when you get into trouble – which happens fairly often! – you are good at finding a way out. Your great weakness is that you often have your eyes fixed so intently on the distant horizon that you fail to notice what’s right in front of your face. You tend to suffer from health problems caused by your own unhealthy habits; your fortunate colors are light blue, light green, and pink. Mars rules this number."

Did you notice anything saying you can't have lasting fulfillment in this life? Neither did I. You'll just have to work at not getting bored and restless with what you achieve.

As for your name number, remember that all the double-digit numbers represent states that have to be achieved -- they don't happen by themselves. If you harness that abundant 5 energy you can achieve the great success that an 11 name number promises; otherwise you'll be a 2, at risk of frittering away your energy and time due to mood swings and unstable enthusiasms.

replies.title: Re: My Birth Number
replies.content: Thank you. I have suffered from mood swings and wasted the past few years of my life because of them.

comments.content:
Could you discuss the differences between discursive meditation and the style of meditation in the essene healing work:

What is the purpose of each?
What are the differences in terms of their long-term effects?
Does the essene healing meditation also help to build the mental body?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Both forms of meditation have the same general benefits that all forms of meditation do, and the Essene healing breath meditation is in its own way fairly generic. Discursive meditation adds systematic mind training to that mix.

2) The difference in their long-term effects is that discursive meditation develops control and clarity of thought to a much greater degree.

3) All forms of meditation build the mental body.

comments.title: Magic and Lust
comments.content:
John Micheal Greer,

Could you tell me some ways to combat the influence of lust? I was also wondering where I might read about the magical nature of lust.

Thankyou as always,

Eamonn

replies.title: Re: Magic and Lust
replies.content:
First of all, "what you resist, persists." The more effort you put into combating lust, the more lust you'll have to combat. Do you recall the Litany against Fear from Dune? It's a more productive approach:

"I must not fear.
Fear is the mind-killer.
Fear is the little-death that brings total obliteration.
I will face my fear.
I will permit it to pass over me and through me.
And when it has gone past, I will turn the inner eye to see its path.
Where the fear has gone there will be nothing. Only I will remain."

Replace "fear" with "lust" and you have a workable approach.

More generally, Catholic writers talk very sensibly about avoiding "the near occasion of sin" -- that is, recognizing those situations that tend to rouse inappropriate desires, and avoiding them. Some of those situations are external, some are internal. With practice, the attitude of the Litany allows you to ignore such things -- you can learn to let a desire pass over you and through you without affecting you -- but until you get there, take steps to avoid things that might stir it up. For example, put one of those parental add-ons on your web browser that won't let it show you explicit content, make the password a long string of random characters, and don't write it down...

replies.title: Re: Magic and Lust
replies.content: Oh, and I meant to say: I don't know of any occult literature dealing with the emotion of lust as such. It's not a big deal to most occultists.

replies.title: Re: Magic and Lust
replies.content:
I see, I thought there might be some writing on lust as an ethereal body but perhaps not.

Thank you for the except from dune. I have never thought to replace “fear” in it but upon doing so I can immediately see that it is a flexible and useful prayer.

Much gratitude to you JMG!


Eamonn

replies.title: Re: Magic and Lust
replies.content:
Getting laid on a regular basis helps, actually, if you have a willing partner. Then, when you are still feeling lustful, you can use the old adage, "It doesn't matter where you get hungry as long as you're coming home for dinner." That helped me when I was in my twenties and thirties and full of lust. I wouldn't want to combat it, because then it will only get entrenched further. Rather channel healthy sexual expression in a natural way.

replies.title: Re: Magic and Lust
replies.content: I've found lust to be similar to gluttony, nervous eating and internet scrolling, dopamine really, which is a two edged sword. At least in me, it is a call to feel good and it seeks manifestation on the basal instincts unless you manage you get yourself out of that state by deep breathing, relaxing and meditation which helps you produce other long lasting and non addictive hormones, like serotonin.

replies.title: Re: Magic and Lust
replies.content:
Building off of what open_space said, often times, lust is not necessarily the lowest-level thing going on, it's more a way to try to address some other issue. Similar to how comfort-eating uses the natural, built-in drive of hunger to motivate you to try to "fix" some other problem by eating, lust might be "comfort-lust" - something is making you feel bad, or you feel like you're lacking something, and some part of you uses the natural, built-in drive for sex to motivate you to try to "fix" whatever that is through sex.

All of which is to say, you might look at less-obvious ways you might be feeling unhappy or unsatisfied besides raw sexual satisfaction, and see if there are other ways to address that. Some common things that folks turn to sex to solve are meaning, validation, excitement, and happiness. Sex can, of course, be a healthy part of finding all of these, the trouble comes in when some part of your mind goes "sex = validation" and starts looking to it as the only way to get what you're after, or the like.

Basically, many folks use what lust drives them to (sex or masturbation) as a kind of self-medication, and just like other kinds of self-medication, there's value in addressing the medicating itself, but there's also value in trying to find other, healthier ways to deal with the "illness."

Maybe none of that rings true, but if it does, hope it helps,
Jeff

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Start by meditating on each planet, sign, and house in the abstract, reviewing their general meanings and getting a sense of how each of them work. Then begin with your first house. Meditate on what it means to have (whatever sign you have) in the 1st. Proceed around the wheel of the houses, considering what each sign brings to the house it rules; look for ways in which these rulerships affect your life.

Then it's on to the planets. Meditate on each planet in its sign and house and see what that tells you. Then meditate on each aspect between planets and see what those tell you. Then try to see your chart as a whole.

Any of these meditations can take more than one session.

comments.title: New Religious Phenomena?
comments.content:
Hi JMG, I’ve noticed a couple possibly related phenomena happening recently and I’m wondering what you think of them.

The first thing is a seemingly huge increase in what looks like fake occultism, just garbled nonsense and fantasy tropes being used by delusional people that think whatever crazy theory they came up with must be true. I would notice such people sometimes over the last few decades, but they seemed like a minority. Now, it seems like they’re flooding online occult spaces to the extent that accurate information is rare, almost as if the internet and the cacophony of nonsense that anyone can post has put the ‘occult’ back in the occult. This all seems fairly recent to me, but maybe I’m just seeing more of it because of the internet and maybe there were always lots of people assuming they could make magic be whatever they could imagine. Or, maybe a real proliferation of fake occultism is happening, driven perhaps by the collapse in public trust in authorities or Neptune in Pisces or the Harry Potter generation or whatever. What do you think?

The related new phenomena, which characterizes a good portion of the above, is what I’ve started calling Neo-christianity. It’s vaguely and shallowly Christian, but mainly focused on massive conspiracy theories about Satanic elites which include literally all fraternal orders, all politicians, all celebrities, etc. Variations seem to include equating Yahweh and Satan, or Jesus and Satan, or Lucifer and Satan and Jesus or whatever, so there’s usually a Gnostic sort of idea going on, but they will deny it because they don’t know anything about that. In every case, it seems assumed that Semitic mythology is the only possible description of the spiritual world, even though they think the evil elites have been ruling the earth consistently for longer than recorded history. Then there’s that combined with the Khazar stuff and sometimes flat-earth. It’s like David Icke’s stuff on steroids, but he’s been reduced to ranting on X about how he is the only one with the truth and everyone else is ‘controlled opposition’.

I never encountered anyone, Christian or otherwise, that seriously believed Freemasons were secret Satanic world rulers or had deep magical secrets until just a few years ago. The Taxil hoax and Satanic Panic have been revived by a new generation that doesn’t know better and has been inoculated against the evidence by Tik Tok videos. So what is this; what’s going on? Is it the second religiosity? Is it a psyop to make people afraid of Freemasonry (especially young men that might want to band together in a failing society), magic, and occultism in general, causing people to see evil everywhere, in every symbol and hand gesture? I know quite a few people in real life that belief these things and I’d like to be able to talk them out of it, but I don’t know how. One person told me that a popular singer is really a Satanic witch AND a Freemason and that her concerts are really rituals to summon demons to possess the audience for some sort of unexplained reason; I guess just because that would be evil if it were even workable. The timing of some of this seems to only have happened after the Qanon thing, like the idea of adrenochrome having life-extension effects, but I never took Qanon seriously so never looked into it. Have you noticed what I’m talking about and what do you think?

Thanks,
Derek Lorian

replies.title: Re: New Religious Phenomena?
replies.content:
Fascinating. I don't spend time on social media so I haven't noticed any increase in fake occultism -- there has always been gargantuan amounts of it, so it may just be finding its way onto TikTok. Still, that's worrisome.

As for the amplification of conspiracy culture, yeah, I've seen some of that, though again not so much since I don't do social media. That said, it may be having an unexpected effect. Here in Rhode Island, at least, we're getting a lot of young men joining Masonic lodges; quite a few of them first heard about Masonry from the conspiracy mongers, spent a while shuddering in pleasurable terror at the supposed enormities of those awful Masons, and then decided it sounded like a lot more fun than anything the fundamentalist churches had to offer and approached their local lodges.

This is nothing new, of course. Every so often, the churches start ranting about the horrible evils of occultism, Masonry, etc., and the result is to bring a flood of new and enthusiastic recruits to occult schools, Masonic lodges, etc. I don't think they've realized that all they're doing is providing us with free advertising...

replies.title: Re: New Religious Phenomena?
replies.content: Interesting, the Austrian writer I mentioned in the open post refers (without citation) to a statistic from 8 years ago that there were more 'energy workers' [Energetiker] in Austria than doctors, and suggests that most are fakes [Illusionisten].

replies.title: Re: New Religious Phenomena?
replies.content:
I've noticed the same thing, and also have friends who profess many different religions and 'beliefs' who have been lost to the various conspiracy bandwagons with as far as I can tell no way to reach them, or at least reaching them is well outside my capabilities. I've found myself in the very odd position of being rejected by both those who accept the commonly held beliefs and also those who claim to reject them. It is a weirdly lonely existence.

After much consideration I've concluded that most (actually, all but a very few) people have a deep seated need for order and structure in the world very much unrelated to their outward choice of religion or spiritual path. It is a deeply unconscious mental refuge for which the myth of Progress had previously fulfilled, but now as Progress continues to falter they grab whatever jumbled mix of 'causes' floats by and desperately coalesce them into some structure that gives meaning to the madness. None of this is in any sense rational nor even coherent, and so the narratives are not either (Progress was not rational either).

I expect the beliefs to get ever more zany and erratic until such time as a new common narrative (or narratives) emerges and then for that to seamlessly replace everything before it - there was a brief (and thankfully temporary) emergence of that with the COVID debacle.

The point at which this finally settles will be the second religiosity, and I think we'll be very fortunate if it ends on something as relatively sane as traditional Christianity. Until then, hang on for a wild ride.

replies.title: Re: New Religious Phenomena?
replies.content:
I know the feeling -- I'm routinely accused of being a crackpot by believers in mainstream culture while being called an ignorant tool of the bad guys by believers in conspiracy culture.

As I see it, there are two common themes that unite the myth of progress and the myth of the One Omnipotant Conspiracy. The first is that they're monolithic: they claim to explain everything under a single narrative. The second is that they both claim that human beings are the most important beings in the cosmos. To progressivists and conspiracists alike, we matter. That shows, I think, the shared terror at the heart of both mythologies: the dreadful realization that the universe doesn't notice our existence and can't care less about us. It's not going to hand us a ticket to a glorious future among the stars and it's not going to grind us under a reptilian bootheel; it's just going to keep on ignoring us.

And until they get around to noticing that this thing they dread has another name, and that name is freedom, they're going to keep on chasing their fantasies.

comments.title: Advice on engaging with Áine
comments.content:
I hesitated to post this, but seeing that this week’s MM starts with Fire, it seemed OK to proceed and ask for advice…

I’m a novice in esoteric matters, having spent most of my life intentionally flying under the radar of the gods, insofar as one can. Respectful, but not involved with them directly.

About a year ago, I started doing the SoP, and have found it a very useful process. I’ve not had any intention of going further and have been content to explore where it takes me.

In the past few months, I’ve encountered some significant challenges in life and career, and have had some unexpected support from an entity that I identify with a minor Irish/Celtic goddess, or faerie queen, Áine (‘awn ya’ in my pronunciation, though I see other variations). A bit of research shows that Áine is associated with radiance, abundance, the sun, and to some extent fire, the context in which I seem to engage with her.

I believe that Áine intervened directly in an important economic matter, the result of which is still uncertain, though in response I’ve created a small shrine on the south wall of my studio with offerings of grain and cinnamon and a Celtic sun symbol as a placeholder for some artwork I’ve yet to create, that will feature a red horse on the left, a salamander elemental on the right, and a radiant sun over water in the middle, framed between two headlands. I contemplate the little shrine and express gratitude for a bit in the morning, before performing the SoP.

I notice the shrine symbolism is a bit mixed, with the salamander perhaps indicating another entity, or reflecting the ambiguous nature of Áine as goddess to some, faerie to others.

All feels right so far: though I am certainly a bit out of my depth, and would welcome any advice you may have on engaging correctly with minor deities and similar entities. What types of action are correct as worship? Are there cautions to observe? Mis-steps to avoid? Any guidance would be much appreciated!

replies.title: Re: Advice on engaging with Áine
replies.content: What you've done so far is classic and wholly appropriate. Syncretism -- the kind of mixed symbolism you're using -- is quite standard, especially in aging civilizations like ours. In terms of your specific questions, worship is a relationship, a very intimate one, and like all really close relationships it's inevitably negotiated by the participants; pay attention and, especially, listen inwardly after you pray, and you'll get the guidance you need.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
First of all, you can't make them show up. They appear when they choose to appear, for reasons of their own. Remember that they really are smarter and more powerful than we are.

Second, the concept of worship has been loaded by the Abrahamic religions with freight it never carried in polytheistic times. The word literally means "worth-ship" -- "respect" or "polite acknowledgment" might be a better translation. The gods of the old polytheist faiths don't want you to grovel.

Third, with this in mind, you might consider getting an image of one or both of the deities you'd like to interact with, making a little shrine (the top of a bookcase or a dresser is suitable), and burning a little incense to them from time to time while letting them know you appreciate their presence in your life. (They are present, of course, whether you sense that presence or not.) That kind of very simple acknowledgment is quite traditional and can lead in various directions, depending on your interests and (much more importantly) what the deity chooses to do.

Not JMG, of course, but if I may, a couple of books you might find helpful: Devotional Polytheism by Galina Krasskova and The Soul's Inner Statues by Kaye Boesme. Both are written to be "generic" for polytheists of any stripe, but Krasskova's focus is Heathenry, and Boesme's is Greek polytheism, so perhaps especially fitting for the two deities you mentioned.

If it's helpful, I wrote up my thoughts on Devotional Polytheism, both its strengths and shortcomings, and included a little bit of my thinking on The Soul's Inner Statues here: https://jpowellrussell.com/#book_thoughts_on_devotional_polytheism

comments.title: Paedrig's Pentacles
comments.content:
So I have had an incredible experience. I decided to try and do a cleansing, focused on cleansing my energy more thoroughly. Which led to the question - what, exactly, do I want to cast out of me? I settled on shame, fear, doubt, and delusion - the first three have crippled me most of my life, with the exception of occasional manic episodes, but it occured to me that somepne without shame or fear could easily become subject to their own delusions, so that seemed like a good way to balance things out.

I charged my talisman with the intention, kept it firmly planted under the obelisk I have in the center of my circle, and proceeded to perform the circle of protection, focusing on driving these four dark spirits. While I drove all away in each direction, with each element I focused on one. With wind to the east I drove out anxiety, with fire to the south I drove out shame, with water to the west I drove out fear, with earth to the north I drove out delusion. I focused on dirivng them away, and visualized them being pressed outside the sphere. At the end, I felt utterly at peace. As though I had acheived perfect calmness in my soul.

The next day, I felt all these malevolent energies all the more powerfully, from the moment I woke up. I focused on the image of the spehere, holding them at bay, yet the more I tried, the harder they attacked. I had an intuitive feeling that I should go cleanse my talisman and other implements - I went into the woods, to wash them in a stream.

As I rinsed everything, I was startled by a passing deer, and dropped my talisman! It bounced away - and then I dropped everything! I scrambled to gather it all up, only to find the coin was missing. I desperately searched, but couldnt find a sign of it. This was the day after christmas, and time ran out before I had to go to work.

All day, I was weighed down, I felt like I had utterly failed, I felt all the darkness crashing in. I also couldnt stop screwing things up at work - all my measurements were off, everything I built seemed off kilter, like something out of bad Dr. Seuss nightmare. Had I made everything worse? Should I have left well enough alone?

Halfway through my shift, something clicked in my mind. Even if I had lost my talisman, even if I had stirred up these dark energies, maybe that was all part of the cleansing process. I couldnt give up or give in! So I pressed through - it wasn't an easy night, but I got everything done. I dragged myself home, exhausted but satisfied.

The next day, I woke up late, refreshed, and with a lightness in my step. I didnt have much time before work, but as soon as I'd had breakfast and coffee I went back out to to the stream. I didnt even have to look - it was right there! I popped my talisman back in my wallet and practically skipped out of the woods.

That day was full of lightness. A rainbow was in the sky, everything was beautiful, and everything went smoothly. I feel like I've really cast away a wright from my soul.

A couple questions - anything you would change about the ritual? Do you recommend I do it again? Should I drive out the same energies, or move on to something else I want to be cleansed of?

Thank you so much for all your guidance!

replies.title: Re: Paedrig's Pentacles
replies.content: My suggestion, predictably enough, is that you should meditate on what happened and learn as much as you can from the experience! I'd also recommend that you leave the whole subject of casting things out alone for a while, and focus on other things. Let the working finish working...

replies.title: Re: Paedrig's Pentacles
replies.content: Predictable yet helpful. I'll let the work work itself out, mediate on this, and continue doing my regular sphere of protection practice. Thanks!

comments.title: Traing the Will
comments.content:
Archdruid,

I've made considerable progress training my Will, since you introduced us to conscious will training several years ago.

One thing I've noticed is that the Will is intensely researched in most university psychology departments. There are several books about the nature of the Will, its function, limitations, and etc...But inevitably all the researchers are forced to make clunky comparisons about the Will to other systems.

One such comparison was the Will's similarity to Muscles. I took that comparison for granted up until about a month ago, when I suddenly realized that although the Will is like a muscle, it does not exactly operate on all the same principals. I'm thinking here specifically of the idea of fatigue. Up until last month I though my Will got fatigued at the end of the day, but then I suddenly that it's not getting fatigued, just following a habit set by the physical body.

The body gets tired, so it must rest. When the will is asleep it follows the habit of the body because the body is a...physical manifestation of the Will? But then when the will wakes up it starts to change the body somehow?

Like when I noticed this last month, I also noticed that my Will didn't get tired, or empty out, but it kinda felt like a loose strand in a ball of yarn. Like something just unraveled that needs to be raveled? So, I set my Will to the task of pulling itself back together and meditated for a short while, and the I felt restored?

Similarly I've started to feel that my desire to go back to sleep is just a habit, and I'm being lazy allowing myself to go back to sleep. So I keep reminding myself that its just a habit of lazyness...which makes sense, because lazyness is just an aversion to action...the Will is active, in motion, even when its sitting still.

Does this track?

replies.title: Re: Traing the Will
replies.content: It does indeed. Excellent -- you've begun a long and very rewarding journey.

comments.title: The Gods
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

Buddhism was mentioned on a thread here tonight, which leads me to a question.

Buddhism tends to have a rather unfavorable view of gods, saying they’re beings caught in samsara just like the rest of us. Therefore, most are unworthy of worship.

I came across a page on Facebook called Platonic Polytheism, run by a gentleman who edited a book of Thomas Taylor’s works. He worships the Greek and Norse deities but also respects other pantheons such as the Hindu. He has the Platonic view that the gods are omniscient, eternal, and prior to nature, and they are worthy of our worship. He says that Buddhism is an impious religion because of its views on gods and also considers it to have a broken worldview.

Seeing that you wrote an excellent book on polytheism, I was wondering your thoughts on this. I’ve done both polytheistic and Buddhist practices, so it would help as far as where to focus.

replies.title: Re: The Gods
replies.content:
Buddhism doesn't speak with a single voice about this. The variety of Buddhism I more or less grew up around, Shingon Buddhism -- one of the two esoteric Japanese denominations -- makes ample room for the worship of gods. Here's a statue of Ganesha, known in Japan as Kangiten, on the grounds of a Japanese Buddhist temple:
[IMAGE:0]
To my mind, it's fair to refer to those branches of Buddhism that reject gods entirely as impious, but it's important not to splash that brush around too broadly. I have a personal devotion to an esoteric Buddhist deity, and I find it to be entirely compatible with my worship of the gods and goddesses.
